steps.message:
You are an AI assistant tasked with solving command-line tasks in a Linux environment. You will be given a task description and the output from previously executed commands. Your goal is to solve the task by providing batches of shell commands.

Format your response as JSON with the following structure:

{
  "analysis": "Analyze the current state based on the terminal output provided. What do you see? What has been accomplished? What still needs to be done?",
  "plan": "Describe your plan for the next steps. What commands will you run and why? Be specific about what you expect each command to accomplish.",
  "commands": [
    {
      "keystrokes": "ls -la\n",
      "duration": 0.1
    },
    {
      "keystrokes": "cd project\n",
      "duration": 0.1
    }
  ],
  "task_complete": true
}

Required fields:
- "analysis": Your analysis of the current situation
- "plan": Your plan for the next steps
- "commands": Array of command objects to execute

Optional fields:
- "task_complete": Boolean indicating if the task is complete (defaults to false if not present)

Command object structure:
- "keystrokes": String containing the exact keystrokes to send to the terminal (required)
- "duration": Number of seconds to wait for the command to complete before the next command will be executed (defaults to 1.0 if not present)

IMPORTANT: The text inside "keystrokes" will be used completely verbatim as keystrokes. Write commands exactly as you want them sent to the terminal:
- You must end every command with a newline (\n) or it will not execute.
- For special key sequences, use tmux-style escape sequences:
  - C-c for Ctrl+C
  - C-d for Ctrl+D

The "duration" attribute specifies the number of seconds to wait for the command to complete (default: 1.0) before the next command will be executed. On immediate tasks (e.g., cd, ls, echo, cat) set a duration of 0.1 seconds. On commands (e.g., gcc, find, rustc) set a duration of 1.0 seconds. On slow commands (e.g., make, python3 [long running script], wget [file]) set an appropriate duration as you determine necessary.

It is better to set a smaller duration than a longer duration. It is always possible to wait again if the prior output has not finished, by running {"keystrokes": "", "duration": 10.0} on subsequent requests to wait longer. Never wait longer than 60 seconds; prefer to poll to see intermediate result status.

Important notes:
- Each command's keystrokes are sent exactly as written to the terminal
- Do not include extra whitespace before or after the keystrokes unless it's part of the intended command
- Extra text before or after the JSON will generate warnings but be tolerated
- The JSON must be valid - use proper escaping for quotes and special characters within strings
- Commands array can be empty if you want to wait without taking action

Task Description:
Need to filter /var/log/app/access.log down to only requests that hit our internal API endpoints — anything matching `/api/v[0-9]+/internal/` — and dump those lines to /home/user/internal_hits.log. But here's the thing: we're also seeing a ton of health check spam from the load balancer that I want excluded, those come from 10.0.0.0/8 range and hit `/health` or `/ready`. So internal API calls yes, but not if they're from the 10.x.x.x health probes.

Also the timestamps in that log are in that annoying Apache combined format with the brackets, would be nice if the output had them as ISO 8601 instead (like 2024-01-15T14:30:22). Rest of the line can stay as-is.

Log's about 50k lines so nothing crazy but I don't want to sit here doing it by hand.


Current terminal state:
Current Terminal Screen:
user@8182679f57e0:~$

Analysis: Starting fresh at the home directory. Need to: 1) Check the access log format, 2) Filter for /api/v[0-9]+/internal/ endpoints, 3) Exclude requests from 10.0.0.0/8 range, 4) Convert Apache combined format timestamps to ISO 8601, 5) Write results to /home/user/internal_hits.log
Plan: First, I'll examine a few lines of the access log to understand its format. Then I'll create a script (likely using awk or a combination of grep and sed/awk) to: 1) Match lines with /api/v[0-9]+/internal/ pattern, 2) Exclude lines with source IPs starting with '10.', 3) Convert timestamps from [DD/Mon/YYYY:HH:MM:SS +ZZZZ] to ISO 8601 format, 4) Output to the target file.

steps.message:
Analysis: Good! I can see the log format is Apache combined format with: IP - - [timestamp] "request" status size "-" "user-agent". I can see examples of health checks from 10.x.x.x IPs hitting /ready, and public API endpoints. Now I need to check if there are actual internal API endpoint entries to understand the full pattern.
Plan: Let me search for lines containing '/api/v' and 'internal' to see what internal API calls look like, then create a filtering script. I'll use awk to: 1) Filter for lines matching /api/v[0-9]+/internal/, 2) Exclude IPs starting with '10.', 3) Convert timestamps from Apache format to ISO 8601.

steps.message:
Analysis: Perfect! I can see internal API calls exist. I notice there are some from 10.x.x.x IPs (like the last one from 10.239.32.97) that need to be excluded. Now I'll create an awk script to: 1) Match /api/v[0-9]+/internal/ patterns, 2) Exclude IPs starting with '10.', 3) Convert timestamps from [DD/Mon/YYYY:HH:MM:SS +ZZZZ] to ISO 8601 format (YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SS).
Plan: I'll create an awk script that processes the log file. The script will: 1) Check if the line contains /api/v[0-9]+/internal/, 2) Check if the IP doesn't start with '10.', 3) Parse and convert the timestamp using month name to number mapping, 4) Reconstruct the line with the new timestamp format. I'll write this as a one-liner or small script.

Analysis: The awk command executed successfully. Now I need to verify that the output file was created correctly and check if it contains the filtered and transformed data.
Plan: I'll check: 1) If the file was created and its size, 2) View a few sample lines to verify the filtering worked (no 10.x.x.x IPs, only internal API endpoints), 3) Verify the timestamp conversion to ISO 8601 format is correct.

steps.message:
Analysis: Excellent! The filtering worked perfectly. The output file contains 4,800 lines (down from 50k), all with: 1) Internal API endpoints matching /api/v[0-9]+/internal/, 2) No IPs starting with '10.' (all IPs shown are from other ranges like 192.168.x.x, 52.x.x.x, 185.x.x.x, 198.x.x.x), 3) Timestamps successfully converted to ISO 8601 format (YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SS). Let me verify that 10.x.x.x IPs were indeed excluded by checking if any remain in the output.
Plan: I'll do a final verification to confirm no 10.x.x.x IPs made it through, and also verify the original log had internal API calls from 10.x.x.x that should have been filtered out.

steps.message:
Analysis: Perfect! All verification checks pass: 1) File exists at /home/user/internal_hits.log with 4,800 lines, 2) Zero IPs starting with '10.' in the output (confirmed twice), 3) All URLs match the /api/v[0-9]+/internal/ pattern correctly (v1, v2, v3 variants with various internal endpoints), 4) All 4,800 timestamps are in valid ISO 8601 format (YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SS), 5) Rest of the log line content is preserved (HTTP method, status codes, user agents, etc.). The task successfully filtered 50k lines down to 4,800 internal API hits, excluding 200 requests from 10.x.x.x range.
Plan: All requirements met. The solution correctly: 1) Filters for /api/v[0-9]+/internal/ endpoints, 2) Excludes all 10.0.0.0/8 IP addresses, 3) Converts Apache combined format timestamps to ISO 8601, 4) Preserves the rest of each log line, 5) Outputs to /home/user/internal_hits.log. Task is complete.
